# School district spending

How much does each district spend to educate a student, and how does Cambridge
compare? This chapter opens with the broadest summary — **current operating
spending per pupil** — and will build toward the major categories of spending in
later sections.

## What "spending per pupil" means here

A district's spending can be measured several ways, and the choice changes the
number a lot. This chapter uses **current operating spending**: the cost of
running schools in a year — salaries, benefits, classroom and BOCES services,
food service, and federally funded programs — divided by **K-12 enrollment**.

It deliberately leaves out two things that make year-to-year comparisons
misleading:

- **Capital projects** (new buildings, roofs, buses) and the **debt** that pays
  for them. These arrive in large, irregular lumps — one building project can
  briefly multiply a district's total spending. Salem's all-funds spending hit
  about \$87,000 per pupil in 2021–22 for this reason, against current operating
  spending of about \$26,000 that year.
- **Transfers between a district's own funds**, which would otherwise be counted
  twice.

This is close to the "current spending per pupil" measure the U.S. Census and the
National Center for Education Statistics use to compare districts and states. The
dollars come from the **New York State Comptroller's** financial-reporting data
and are **not adjusted for inflation**, so part of the upward drift over time
reflects rising prices rather than rising real resources.

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import os
import sys

sys.path.insert(0, os.getcwd())

import polars as pl
from plotnine import (
    aes,
    geom_line,
    geom_point,
    ggplot,
    labs,
    scale_color_manual,
    scale_x_continuous,
    scale_y_continuous,
)
from great_tables import GT, loc, md, style

import comparisons as cc

# Format axis/break values as whole dollars.
def _dollars(breaks):
    return [f"${v:,.0f}" if v is not None else "" for v in breaks]
```

## Spending per pupil, over time

**Cambridge spends near the top of the group per pupil — consistently second only to Salem.**

```{python}
#| label: fig-spending-per-pupil
#| fig-cap: "Current operating spending per K–12 pupil (nominal dollars) by school year ending, 2016 (2015–16) through 2025 (2024–25). Source: NYS Comptroller local-government financial data; K-12 enrollment from NYSED."
g = (
    cc.spending_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    .filter(pl.col("year_end") >= 2016)
    .select("district", "year_end", "current_pp")
    .drop_nulls()
)
cambridge = g.filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)

(
    ggplot(g, aes("year_end", "current_pp", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=cc.PALETTE, name="", limits=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2016, 2026)))
    + scale_y_continuous(labels=_dollars)
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Current operating spending per pupil",
        title="Current operating spending per K-12 pupil, 2015–16 to 2024–25",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)
```

The two highest-spending districts per pupil — Cambridge and Salem — are also the
two **smallest** by enrollment. That is the cost-side mirror of the
[students-per-teacher](class-size-all-measures.qmd) picture: a small district spreads fixed
costs (a principal, a building, a bus run) over fewer students, so its spending
*per pupil* runs high even when its total budget is modest. Every district's
figure rises over the decade, but remember these are nominal dollars — much of
that climb is inflation.

## The full comparison group

The table widens the lens to all twelve table-group districts. Columns are
school-years-ending; the final column is the change from 2016 to 2025 (in nominal
dollars). Cambridge is highlighted.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-spending-per-pupil
#| tbl-cap: "Current operating spending per K–12 pupil by district and school year ending (nominal dollars)."
t = cc.spending_for(cc.TABLE_GROUP).filter(pl.col("year_end").is_between(2016, 2025))

wide = (
    t.select("region", "district", "year_end", "current_pp")
    .with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("region") == "Washington County")
        .then(0)
        .otherwise(1)
        .alias("_rank")
    )
    .pivot(values="current_pp", index=["_rank", "region", "district"], on="year_end")
    .sort(["_rank", "district"])
    .drop("_rank")
)

year_cols = [c for c in wide.columns if c.isdigit()]
wide = wide.with_columns((pl.col("2025") - pl.col("2016")).alias("chg"))

(
    GT(wide, rowname_col="district", groupname_col="region")
    .tab_header(
        title="Current operating spending per pupil",
        subtitle="School-year-ending 2016 (2015–16) through 2025 (2024–25), nominal dollars",
    )
    .fmt_currency(columns=year_cols + ["chg"], decimals=0)
    .tab_spanner(label="School year ending", columns=year_cols)
    .cols_label(chg=md("Change<br>2016→2025"))
    .sub_missing(missing_text="—")
    .tab_style(
        style=[style.fill(color="#FCEFB4"), style.text(weight="bold")],
        locations=[
            loc.body(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
            loc.stub(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
        ],
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        md(
            "**Source:** New York State Comptroller local-government financial "
            "data (expenditures); K-12 enrollment from NYSED. Current operating "
            "spending = General, Special Aid and School Lunch funds, excluding "
            "capital outlay, debt service and interfund transfers. Nominal "
            "dollars; year = school-year-ending (2025 = 2024–25)."
        )
    )
)
```

Across the wider group the same pattern holds: the smallest districts tend to
post the highest spending per pupil, and Cambridge sits near the top. Later
sections will open up *what* the money buys — the major categories of spending —
and add the general-fund-only and total-all-funds views for context.
